posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I am buying Wasenhaus! The best Burgundy your money can buy. And Made in Germany.

William - How is 18 for whites?

2018 whites are defined by the high yields. That retarded ripening, despite the warm conditions, so alcohol levels are generally moderate (e.g. <2017). Acidities are a touch below average but more or less correct. What really differentiates the top wines from the rest is concentration: many 2018 whites are a bit soft, open-knit, fluid. Nature was so generous that people who generally crop high exceeded the AOC maximums, and those who are normally more restrained often attained them. The best producers made efforts to restrain their yields. And they did their classic press cycles, so the wines have a bit of extract. Some producers, by contrast, had so much fruit to process that they did extra short press cycles, resulting in very clear juice (no skin extract or solids), which exaggerates the perception of dilution. If you think back to the very high yielding great vintages of the past for white Burgundy such as 1979 and 1982, one of the real differences with 2018 (despite similar yields) is that today people have pneumatic presses than can work really quickly and extract almost nothing from the grapes. Back in the late 1970s, people were crushing the grapes and then pressing with vertical basket presses or vaslins, which took longer and resulted in a lot more extract to structure the wines. I think this is one of the reasons why there were more great 1982 white Burgundies than there are great 2018 white Burgundies. But we will see how they evolve. There are some very nice wines out there, including but not limited to those that I purchased. You just have to be selective - but what’s new about that!

posts.name: Joel Galang
posts.cooked:
For people responding to this thread, I’d be curious to know how you make your decisions if you haven’t tasted the wines? Following the press, this forum, word of mouth, or simply trying to maintain allocations with sources?

I am not a huge / major player in buying red Burgundy, but it is by far the largest percentage of my cellar. Buying for me is almost 100% based on reputation and tastings of previous vintages from the same producer. By the time I get offers via email, the wine I am buying is mostly pre-arrival, there is no way for me to taste it. I do not buy heavy hitters like DRC, Roumier, etc. but the producers I buy do still sell out pretty quickly. La Paulee is not useful as well as by the time you taste those wines, most of it has been sold already and what is left are a lot more expensive after market prices (if any). This is especially true of good vintages, bad vintages like 2004 will of course linger, but in recent vintages (starting from 2015 vintage), things tend to sell out a lot more quickly (at least the ones with decent pricing).

In short, I do not get to taste what I am buying, but made my decisions by a mix of tasting previous vintages (75%) from that producer (I was part of 2 monthly tasting groups, one solely focused on Burgundy, for a good number of years, pre-COVID of course), plus this forum (25%), 0% allocation based (I guess I do not buy heavy enough?).

Interesting. Why are you not influenced by La Paulee tastings? You definitely get the style of the producer and the general way they go about producing their wines. They are often there in person to talk to directly and you can get questions answered about the vintages. Perhaps the particular wines you taste may be more expensive, but it is always helpful to verify if you like the wines style directly before making a buying decision. I found out at the last Paulee (seems like a 1,000 years ago) that if I get any Lafarge wines I need to bury them really deep in the cellar as I don’t care for the early rustic nature of the wines and the obvious tannic structure. On the other hand I could drink most of the Mugneret Gibourg wines immediately and get a lot of pleasure. Found it helpful, but clearly a bit hard to separate wines after a while in those mass events.

I might have not explained it to well, yes if you attend La Paulee you get a better sense of the producers, their style and vintage characteristics (assuming you only got for the grand tasting). However, it’s a timing thing for me, for example, by the time La Paulee rolls over, most of the wine I am tasting for that vintage have already been offered (and sold out) months prior. It can be useful for the following vintage (not the one being poured at La Paulee), but as strictly being useful for buying in the “current” vintage, it is not useful for me for reasons just explained. There are exceptions of course and this is just a personal preference for me. If one has deep enough pockets, one can surely buy almost anything in the secondary market, unfortunately due to limited resources, I have to go in blind when buying current vintages and only rely on prior experiences with the producer and board posts as a guide especially since Burgundy prices have gone up quite a lot since I started buying.

For example, Mugneret Gibourg Vosne Romanee started out as $60 when I started buying, was easier to pop one before for testing but now at $160+, it is much harder to pop one for the sake of tasting (especially since I can only afford to buy 2-3 bottles max nowadays). I just have faith that the excellence of these current vintages follow the wines of M-G that I had before.

I buy the vast majority of my Burgundy en primeur so actually made most of my buying decisions in December 2019/ January 2020 for the 2018s before most reviews had been released. When you have allocations of things you can’t wait for the reviews to come out. Even if I could wait for reviews, if I use Barthod as an example, I typically can’t cherry pick which premier crus I get based on what outperformed that year - I get the ones I’ve been buying every year. Sometimes the ones I buy outperform versus her other wines and sometimes they don’t.

I follow your and other Burgundy critics’ views and will buy wines that are available at reasonable prices on the secondary market that have been well reviewed - I just picked up a 6 pack of 2017 Vougeraie Clos de Vougeot for a reasonable price off the back of a nice note from you (and a corresponding one from Allen). However, typically I’ll use professional reviews (and comments from contributors here) of wines from the previous 3-5 vintages to inform views of what to buy in the next vintage. It’s not perfect but it definitely gives an idea of what producers are substantially improving the quality of wines they make and what I should try to buy for the next vintage.

posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
For people responding to this thread, I’d be curious to know how you make your decisions if you haven’t tasted the wines? Following the press, this forum, word of mouth, or simply trying to maintain allocations with sources?

I have two answers. When I first started collecting Burgundy over 30 years ago, before I had experience and preferences, I would buy a bottle of something that interested me from reading Coates or Tanzer, the only two reliable timely Burgundy reviewers, or from a trusted merchant, bring the bottle home, try it within a week or two and then, if I liked it, go back and buy more. Wine stayed on the shelves—Ramonet, Roumier, Raveneau, even DRC, weeks or months.

Wine-Searcher didn’t exist. Relationships were paramount.

Fast forward to the release of 2005 reds and it all changed. Huge reviews and lots of new wine money showed up. Relationships meant nothing. Wine was sold to the highest bidder. Long time allocations vaporized as many retailers turned to the new clientele waving their dollars.

Since then, the supply demand curves have been so twisted so that one has to buy without tasting, bottles and not cases, and one doesn’t have the leisure of trying and tasting.

I miss the previous times.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
For people responding to this thread, I’d be curious to know how you make your decisions if you haven’t tasted the wines? Following the press, this forum, word of mouth, or simply trying to maintain allocations with sources?

So a big part is for sure keeping allocations (for me DRC, Rousseau, Roumier, Trapet, Dujac, Vogue, Leflaive, Bachelet).

Above that is a layer of research, going through all the articles of the pros (your material on TWA, Neil’s on Vinous, Brughound, Winehog and Decanter are my main sources) to see if the vintage is aligned with my preferences or not, if the vintage is a good one or not. Depending on that I increase or lower my allocation requests.

Regarding other wines: As I’m relatively young (mid thirties) and compared to Bdx or Napa my Burgundy knowledge and drinking history is relatively thin (you can hardly buy back vintages to tast and learn the same way you can do in Bdx) I don’t have 10 other producers I know well enough to buy them regurarly. So my way to decide which wines to buy, beside the very big names, depends on the rating and comments you, Neil and the others make. Not knowing the producers that well, not having had so much Burgundy to know which critic’s palate aligns most with mine, I usually seek out the wines that a) have good ratings from more than one critic, b) a history of being good (critics, cellartracker). In 2018 I bought Angerville Clos de Ducs, Groffier CdB, Faiveley CdB, Anne Gros Richebourg, Rossignol-Trapet Chambertin. My “1er Cru/Village pruchases” are mainly replaced with buying high end Pinot from Germany (Huber, Becker, Stodden, Fürst) and Switzerland (Studach, Donatsch, Gantenbein).

He basically starts from the premise that you have to like the new style of the wines, and if you don’t you should move on but if you do then there are some great wines (particularly in ‘19, it sounds like). I thought it was nice to hear Jasper start from the premise that we are in a new paradigm and it is unlikely to go back to the old.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
For people responding to this thread, I’d be curious to know how you make your decisions if you haven’t tasted the wines? Following the press, this forum, word of mouth, or simply trying to maintain allocations with sources?

I have two answers. When I first started collecting Burgundy over 30 years ago, before I had experience and preferences, I would buy a bottle of something that interested me from reading Coates or Tanzer, the only two reliable timely Burgundy reviewers, or from a trusted merchant, bring the bottle home, try it within a week or two and then, if I liked it, go back and buy more. Wine stayed on the shelves—Ramonet, Roumier, Raveneau, even DRC, weeks or months.

Wine-Searcher didn’t exist. Relationships were paramount.

Fast forward to the release of 2005 reds and it all changed. Huge reviews and lots of new wine money showed up. Relationships meant nothing. Wine was sold to the highest bidder. Long time allocations vaporized as many retailers turned to the new clientele waving their dollars.

Since then, the supply demand curves have been so twisted so that one has to buy without tasting, bottles and not cases, and one doesn’t have the leisure of trying and tasting.

I miss the previous times.

I know a fair amount of people who have been buying Burgundy for a long time who feel this way, but I also know a fair number of people new to Burgundy who believe that older buyers have allocations at really cheap prices. I think the answer is in between and relationships very much still matter, but to a point. For example, I don’t have the right relationships for Tremblay, and those wines simply don’t appear at retail - I can’t buy them even if I wanted to. Same is true for certain other producers. Whereas I do have relationships for certain other producers, so get offered things that certain other people don’t.

What I think people who have been buying for a long time lament is the ability to peruse available wine and buy them at their leisure without price mark-ups, but that’s simply the nature of the price increase in Burgundy, not relationships ceasing to matter. They still do; just try and buy Burlotto’s 16s, for example.

I am with alan on this. Partly because I feel like the ready availability via internet of “scores” etc has made the time i spent tasting, reading, talking and learning burgundy has been rendered worthless. When I had to start competing with newbies who had no idea whether they would like a producer or a cru but bought heavily because allen meadows or chet kern or even howard cooper (!) raved up a burg on a wine board, the time, effort and experience I had gained meant nothing.

But I sure wish I had allocations at cheap prices. I always paid the going rate - it was just that the going retail rate wasn’t inflated by too much money in too few hands + newly available internet-gained knowledge.

I don’t really agree with this - much of the benefit you get is in not buying bad wine. (Or, to be more accurate, wine that is bad to your palate.) There’s no substitute for the experience of your own palate - there’s plenty of wine Howard likes that I don’t, regardless of his experience. Chet obviously knows vastly more about white burgundy than I do, but I’d never buy things just because he likes them - lots of people whose palates I admire like things I do not.

I think what people are lamenting is the scarcity driven price increase, driven in part by a lot more knowledge. Which, obviously is true. But some of that will definitely lead to people buying a lot of wine they will discover in later years they don’t really want to drink. And some of it is a bit like being annoyed that the band you used to have to pay $20 to see in a dive bar when you were young now charges $150 to play in sold our stadia.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It was really interesting to hear Jasper basically draw a line between pre-2017 and the ‘18/‘19/‘20 (and presumably future) style of Burgundian wines. He basically starts from the premise that you have to like the new style of the wines, and if you don’t you should move on but if you do then there are some great wines (particularly in ‘19, it sounds like). The typical critical move is to say “it’s a combination of these two prior vintages”, which I’ve always found unhelpful. I thought it was nice to hear Jasper start from the premise that we are in a new paradigm and it is unlikely to go back to the old.
A

In the last Burghound report, Alan touches on a lot of the same facts as Jasper (yields, temp, precipitation, etc.) but in describing the whites he doesn’t latch onto a paradigm shift the way Jasper does. He’s a bit more restrained, referring to the season as “surprising” and results as “a mix of 2016 and 2017”.

I find my palate aligns more these days with J Morris than Alan. When you get producers talking about getting EtOH levels of 14.5, 15.5, or 16 from some parcels, over three consecutive years, THAT is a paradigm shift. He also stated there were very few producers who chaptalized to any great degree in '18. To say this was a vintage that combined '16 and '17 is not helpful. Morris also has specific data in the podcast, with precise rainfall amounts village by village, and corresponding tasting impressions. This is combined with the fact that Burghound is a little timid for me in calling out up and comers and rating sites more individually. Sorry, but I get more out of the JM stuff…

This post, and others, raise some very interesting questions—and tangents—for discussion. In fact, most of the below is a tangent about the 2019 vintage in the middle of a thread on the 2018 vintage, but here goes. For everyone’s reference, here are the rainfall statistics in 2019 by commune, as collated by the Chambre d’Agriculture of the Côte d’Or:


2019 Côte d’Or Rainfall by Commune by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

While interesting, to my mind, this data is of limited utility for understanding the 2019 vintage. For a start, the distribution is actually rather small. But, more importantly, it tells you nothing about how the vines experienced these conditions: for example, I would rather have had old vines on drought resistant rootstocks in deep soils with a favorable hydrological profile in dry Marsannay than young vines on drought-susceptible rootstocks in thin soils in (comparatively) wet Morey. Viticulture and soil management play a huge role in modulating vines’ susceptibility to drought. If one does not know the parcel-by-parcel conditions and viticultural approaches, one cannot begin to speculate about the impact of even comparatively significant disparities in rainfall (which most of these commune-by-commune differences in 2019 are not: 8% between Monthélie and Chassagne, for example). And then, in terms of determining the actual style of the wine in the glass, in a vintage such as 2019 picking date is decisive: Ghislaine Barthod, one of the earliest to pick in Chambolle-Musigny, and François Bertheau, one of the latest, will have both had the same rainfall in their parcel of Chambolle Charmes; yet the resulting wines are very different. So to cite this sort of data as if it were a critical heuristic for understanding the vintage is mistaken, I think, and also risks taking us back to the sort of reductionist vintage generalizations of yesteryear that proclaimed that such-and-such a vintage was “a great vintage for Gevrey” or whichever commune. The reality is that quality is determined in the vines on a parcel-by-parcel level, and in the cellars on a producer-by-producer level. And there is no one metric that provides the key for making sense of complex, holistic systems determined by a multitude of interrelated variables. Now, this is not intended as a rebuke to Jasper, and we all have different ways of making sense of the region and the data. But I would say that, in the glass, just to take two examples, I didn’t find Cyril Audoin’s 2019 Marsannays any less fresh and dynamic than Jeremy Seysses’ wines at Domaine Dujac in Morey–irrespective of that fact that those two communes site at opposite ends of the chart above.

The interesting tangent here, to my mind, is the broader question about the place for data in wine criticism. I see more and more reviews, especially of Bordeaux wines, that give pHs, IPTs and other numbers. These are indeed objective “facts” about the wines, but what information can actually be derived from them? I tend not to record this sort of information, unless a wine is obviously defined in the glass by for example a very high or very low pH, or a very high level of alcohol—and the numbers confirm this.

I also can’t resist getting into the question of paradigm shifts. If anyone wanted to talk about drawing lines in 1989 and 2003, they would have my endorsement, but it seems to me a little premature to draw a line after 2017. 2003, from before the division, remains more extreme in terms of temperature extremes, accumulation, and number of consecutive days >100 F than 2020, 2019 or 2018. 2015, 2009 and (arguably) 2005 would also sit quite easily, to my mind, in the company of 2020, 2019 and 2018 in terms of conditions and, making allowances for changing winemaking approaches, wine styles. To situate things in a deeper context, back in the 18th century, Dom. Dénise wrote that the best wines in Burgundy were generally made in vintages with mid-September harvests: well, that was the case in 2019! So let’s see what the next few years bring, and let’s not give up on Burgundy just yet.

The remark that producers have also learnt to make fresher wines in extreme vintages is also very true. I have hesitated to get into this subject, as I do not want to be type-cast as an “apologist” for super-ripe vintages: for the record, many of the most profound Burgundies I have drunk were wines made from what would today be considered marginally ripe fruit, and the aromatic range of cool, late vintages is very hard to march. I have more 2016s and 2017s in my cellar than 2015s and 2018s. But, it clearly is possible to make much “fresher” wines in warm vintages by picking early in the morning, using refrigerated trucks to keep fruit cool, adapting vinification and élevage practices. 2003s were mostly vinified like 2002s, 2001s, 2000s etc before them, and that often didn’t work. 2020s, by contrast, were made with the benefit of accumulated experience of a handful of very warm vintages, and it really does show. So be cautious about over-extrapolating.

Adaptation in the vines and wineries is clearly possible: Bertrand Dugat at Domaine Claude Dugat barely exceeded 13% in 2018, 2019 and 2020—and his wines in these years are beautifully refined, perfumed and pure (FYI, several of his 2017s were a hair below 12%). His vineyard work is impeccable and he timed his harvest carefully. Because some producers failed to adapt and made 16% monsters, or perhaps even wanted to make 16% monsters, do not infer that this was unavoidable or that it needs to be the new normal. Picking over-ripe fruit is a choice, even if it’s harder to dial in on the perfect date in a warm, sunny year when everything moves very fast.

posts.name: D Borczon
posts.cooked: Interesting and granular, as always. However the paradigm shift, in my post, is that more likely in the near future is the need for Burgundians to adjust for heat and dry conditions more often than wet and cool. Positive things for some issues, like disease, but more negative for other aspects of vineyard management. Point in the larger sense is perhaps the real survivors who will be the innovative and flexible will outdistance those who may persist in doing things “because my grandfather did it”. You cannot deny Msr Kelley that bits of well timed rain in August may be the razors edge difference in some vineyards doing better than another. Producers are able to adjust for many things these days, but watering thirsty grapes is something the Burgundians are still not (yet) permitted to do.

I do not deny that as a general observation, but with regards to the 2019 vintage in particular, you will note that all the communes in the data above received some rain in August! So it is not the case that some saw rain and others didn’t.

I agree, however, about the paradigm: if in the past, it was the producers who were able to ripen grapes in adverse conditions who succeeded the best; now it’s those who succeed in capturing freshness in warm conditions. And if that was the paradigm under discussion—rather than the notion that post-2017 Burgundy tastes inherently and irreversibly different from pre-2017 Burgundy—then I can warmly get behind it!

I think there has been just as big a “paradigm shift”, if you like, in consumer culture during the same period, by the way. If 2005 were to come along today, I think its quality would be interrogated a lot more vigorously (as should the quality of every vintage, in my opinion!) than was the case back in 2006-7. Ten years ago, the notion that Burgundy might get a bit riper would likely not have prompted anyone to ask who the “survivors” might be, to borrow your term!

As a fairly new burgundy buyer, threads list this on this forum are incredibly helpful - and provide insight that reading scores/tasting notes definitely do not. For example, as someone who doesn’t get to taste before buying, and does not have the option of buying and going back for more (since they will all be gone), I almost exclusively use this board and tasting notes/scores of those whom I trust.

Without threads like this, I would be incredibly confused. For example, I use William’s notes and words more than any other. Even he, when comparing 2017 to 2018, seems to have given higher scores in 2018 vs. 2017 to numerous of the wines I buy year in and year out (i.e. 94/96 v. 92/94 for d’Angerville Clos des Ducs). Without generous input from members on this thread and William’s additional input, I would have a hard time deciphering how much of what vintages to buy.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
For example, I use William’s notes and words more than any other. Even he, when comparing 2017 to 2018, seems to have given higher scores in 2018 vs. 2017 to numerous of the wines I buy year in and year out (i.e. 94/96 v. 92/94 for d’Angerville Clos des Ducs).

This is a very fair point! As a set, in retrospect, I think I somewhat underrated the 2017 reds. Having worked the harvest and seen the high yields in the vineyards, with potential alcohols in the high elevens and low twelves at harvest and rather overcast weather around harvest, I was a bit skeptical. Tasting from barrel a year later, I frequently found the wines a touch fluid and diffuse. Now, there are plenty of fluid and diffuse 2017 reds out there; but the top producers’ wines mostly took on additional depth with a second winter on the lees. And, revisiting from bottle, I have simply adored the wines’ suppleness and charm and definition. And at this stage, I have reviewed just shy of 2,000 2017s in bottle!

This experience has informed a number of reflections. The first is that, in Burgundy, sometimes the prettiest fruit doesn’t make the best wines. The second it how important it is to revisit the wines in bottle, something I do more and more systematically. The third is to question why it is that the more powerful wines tend to be those that get the highest scores. Rather, shouldn’t the highest scores go to the wines that we most want in our cellars? This isn’t just a Burgundian phenomenon, far from it—the ratings of TBA Riesling are the perfect example. But should 2017 Burgundies really be marked down because they are, in many cases, 30-40 year wines rather than 60-70 year wines? As a profession, this is a question wine writers really need to get to grips with, especially as, in northern European wine regions, power becomes easier and easier to achieve, and it is certainly front-and-center in my mind when I am tasting new vintages along the Côte these days.

posts.cooked:

As a profession, this is a question wine writers really need to get to grips with, especially as, in northern European wine regions, power becomes easier and easier to achieve, and it is certainly front-and-center in my mind when I am tasting new vintages along the Côte these days.

Thank you for this! It would be fantastically useful if critics were more focused on the consumers needs in this way.

Re revisiting wines in bottle – everyone buying the wine will drink it from bottle! I see a certain minority of Burgundy TNs that read like “I tasted this early on and it was so reduced I couldn’t really smell much or pick out the flavors, but it sure was big and I like the producer so I’m sure it will come together/turn out great”. Not helpful.

On overscoring more powerful/long aging wines – this is HUGE problem. Speaking as a 53 year old with what I have to assume is a finite life span (although hope springs eternal), when I buy new releases I am specifically looking to AVOID a multi-decade wrestling match with tannins and other forms of orneriness that will lead to my opening bottles that are still “too young” 15 years after the vintage date. And I don’t think I’m at all unusual in that respect. For me at this point in my life it’s actually a plus to have a charming but seemingly “smaller” vintage that I can drink at an earlier and more predictable level of aging. What I really want is for critics to help me differentiate between vintages that are “smaller” and will drink younger but have real charm and attractiveness, vs vintages that are clunky and dried out and will lack charm even when they mature. Critics do not do a good job at that at all. In general, despite some improvements, vintage assessments are still too unidimensional “great/good/mediocre/bad”, usually along an implicit fruit intensity scale, and should be much more multidimensional and descriptive of the particular pluses and minuses of the year.

Also, critical drinking windows/aging forecasts in general are a mess, which I can understand since it’s so hard to predict, but it’s one of the areas where being a professional wine drinker should give the biggest relative advantage in forecasting so critics should try to do it better.

My sentiments exactly. After tasting and talking to producers at La Paulee this year, tasting the 17s, I am happy to have many of them in my cellar. They will provide pleasurable drinking in the nearer term. At my age I hardly need another 05 vintage to sit on. (I still fear to open the village wines in that year!)
Many thanks again to WK. Maybe he deserves his own abbreviation name, sort of like Ocasio-Cortez. To further provoke him, might I suggest he consider a blind tasting of 18 Red Burg’s vs 18 Sonoma Coast and Russian River Pinots? Now that would be instructive blind!!!

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
It all comes back to balance. Complexity and intensity are also major factors, but if you don’t have balance, you fall. I have not tasted 2017 Burgundies, but I gather they are a far better version of 2007s which I am drinking with a good deal of pleasure now, although a few bottles have surprised me, and I will drink their brethren in a decade.

I do however remain leery of all these vintage pronouncements. Gone are the days when consumers bought wines based on general vintage notes and their experience with particular chateaux. We have dozens of critics running round tasting, with each having a different take on every major wine produced. We no longer need to worry about vintages per se, except to know where the possible lies. Yes it is useful to know that Gevrey had hailstones the size of baseballs on June 23rd, but how often have I heard that those same hailstones ruined someone’s neighbor’s vines but narrowly missed his. And sometimes, the wine actually reflects that.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I think 18,19, and 20 will go the same way as 05, praised to the heavens by the critics and then a fair percentage purchased by wealthy Burgundy neophytes who believe the critics drinking windows, my experience has been that these ripe vintages tend to shut down hard and hibernate for a loong time and can be very frustrating to own.

I don’t think so! On the one hand, the press has been much more circumspect about 2018 than 2005 (which many commentators proclaimed as “the greatest of my career” etc), and I think the general line has been that this is an extreme vintage in which some very good wines were made—which seems to me quite a coherent assessment of the situation. On the other, winemaking is less extractive and oak better chosen today than was the case in 2005, and that, combined with more fully mature tannins, means that I think the 2018 wines are going to be suppler and more open throughout their lives than has been the case with the 2005s. (I might add that people are also a bit more circumspect with throwing tartaric acid around, which was always a less talked about factor in why ripe vintages shut down hard in the past: many 2005s were chaptalized and saw important tartaric adds).

It is also important not to lump 2018, 2019 and 2020 together. They are very different vintages. 2019 was a mid-September harvest, remember!

I can only underline that this is a choice. Charles Lachaux’s 2019 Echézeaux is around 13%; Emmanuel Rouget’s is 16.2%. The appellation and vintage are the same but the wines are very different. So, a lot of what we ascribe to vintage character is actually the result of producers’ choices.

posts.name: Mikael B E K E L E
posts.cooked:
With a bit of time now, you bought the 2018 and if you tried any bottles what are your thought?

I am trying entry level things from the likes of Montille, Hudelot- Baillet, Confuron (only 1er cru - but this really needs more time and the barrel tannins have not integrated, yet) and in my mind before opening the bottles I kind of expected round warm year wines. Drinking them after two nights what strikes me (again a small sample) is that it’s not a warm wine profile necessarily. I somehow think of Nebbiolo in the austerity with both tannins and acidity (yet with moderate alcohol) and with a good level of fruit powering through. I love Nebbiolo so that’s not a negative but and I think those qualities usually favors aging.

Can’t help thinking that even these “humbler” in burgundy terms could turn out to be quite something with a bit more time. They just keep growing in presence and complexity (day two now).

Ps 1. I don’t have much more exciting in the cellar for 2018 - just curious.

Ps 2. I have had simpler white burgundy that shows quite a lot of freshness and are quite a bit more exciting than I would have expected from some type of aggregated general impressions shared here and there.
